author: @justinw1765
text:
Combine with insulation like high quality goose down,  the new polymer aerogels, or vacuum insulation*, and one might be able to make an actual practical device?  
      Another thing that works is at night, make a conical IR reflector--foam or cardboard with Al foil or the like, invert it, put the small diameter end in a black pot, and point to the open night sky.  It will radiate more IR energy up into space and cool down the black pot or whatever might be in the black pot.  From what I've heard, it can cool down around 15 degrees F pretty easily.  Put that cold water in a plastic bag, or stone, in your fridge, and you can help to jumpstart the cooling process.  
     But honestly, it probably just makes the most sense to dig down into the earth and insulate the top hole cover.  Don't remember the name, but there is a place in AZ (or NM?) in the US where there is a cave that has perpetual ice, even though the temps may be 90 or more *F relatively near the cave--which btw is not that deep in like you might think.

replies.author: @justinw1765
replies.text:
Btw, anyone have any suggestions for the following?   I was thinking that to make some lower cost and lower weight vacuum insulation panels, you could use a combo of strong per weight plywood treated with S2 fiberglass cloth/epoxy, and in between the wood/S2/epoxy panels, put a few strong, rigid, low thermal conductivity closed cell foam like EEP as short support pillars between the two pieces (like 3 spaced apart). 
   But the question is, once you have drawn the vacuum say through a drilled hole, how does one seal it effectively (and you would probably have to do 6 individual panels separately)?   
   If you could do this, it would make like the ultimate cooler!  (especially if combined with an IR reflector inside or outside).

replies.text: It does sound like with the correct materials,, fan cooling of the super coiling, and a ratchet system for the coiling, which would release when the coil reaches ambient temperature, and then a brief fan burst pushing air into the bottom of the cooling chamber, it might be quite effective. Given heat rises the top should allow for the expulsion of air, which is why I was thinking the cool air should be blown into the bottom. It would be interesting to see the premise properly developed, if at all possible

author: @jjmcrosbie
text:
Sir, I found that very interesting - as are all of your videos, thank you.
1 - Refrigeration in general - I believe - is traditionally performed by adding energy to a system at one place, and taking it out in another. 
2 - As a parallel to the mechanocaloric effect, I would compare a cylinder containing a piston and air.  You compress the air with the piston and it gets hotter than ambient - due to the energy input.  You let it cool back to ambient, then allow the air to expand back to atmospheric pressure and it goes colder than ambient.  If I wanted to make a fridge that way, I'd  pipe the cold air somewhere else, eg into a closed container containing such material as I wanted to refrigerate.  That seems practical if not very efficient.
3 - Are there practical methods available for performing similar movements of the refrigerant (rubber or etc) in the mechanocaloric method? or magneto- or electro-  ?  Or maybe we move the item to be cooled instead?  How is the refig. material insulated while it's going through it heat-up phase?
4 - Is the twisted rubber (or etc) method more efficient than the air cylinder?  It seems to me there must be energy loss in some kind of friction within the material.
Thank you again for this video.  Clive.

replies.author: @ChristmasEve777
replies.text: You can't cool it back down to room temperature in the same action that you're taking to cool below room temperature (unwinding it).  You'll just end up with a rubber band that's right around room temperature again - not below.  It has to be cooled back down prior to unwinding.   Energy is released nonetheless so, as Robert said, that could be recovered.

author: @kreynolds1123
text:
Using electrostatics and an elastomer for mechanical caloric effect cooling: Google  Dielectric  elastomer.... 

Instead of mechanically stretching an elastomer, one can mechanically compress it with electrostatic forces. That is because the rubber does not change volume but its surface area changes under both compression and tension aligning the elastomer chains along a direction. 

Paint graphene ink on two sides of a thin rubber sheet and apply a high voltage between the two plates. The area of the two plates will grow as the graphene plates are squeezed together because of the electrostatic forces. The elastomer will loose excess heat energy during compression phase and absorb heat on the relax phase.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that is certainly one whole field to look at mate - but it doesn't make it better - it just makes it different. Mechanical systems have their own place and can very much be a solution.

author: @kreynolds1123
text:
Here is an idea I've been kicking around. Dielectric elastomer to electrostatic induction pump electrons. 1) Paint both sides of a rubber sheet in graphene ink. 2) Place a static high voltage on one plate and connect the other to ground through a load. 3) Stretching the rubber increases its length and surface area while distance between the plates shrinks as the overall volume of the elastomer stays the same. This means the capacitance changes as you stretch and relax the elastomer.  

An Electrostatic charge on one plate through electrostatic induction induces an opposing charge on the other plate when electrons are free to move to and or from ground and the other plate. Alternatly Changing capacitance would electro static induction pump electrons to and from ground driving alternating currents through a load.

author: @alaingrignon
text: Interesting video. Could a number of bands be used in tandem to accelerate cooling? Band 1, 2, and 3 are supercoiled and brought to room temp, in a box. Band1 unravels, cooling the temperature in the box as well as bands 2 and 3 (still supercoiled), band 2 unravels cooling the box further, and band 3. Band 3 uncoils while band 1 and 2 and recoiled in the cooled box. etc... etc..

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: for sure mate - check the papers I mention and look around them this has been made into working devices
